---
title: "Convex Sets"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Optimization"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article,
# matched to the rest of the series.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

REGION = "#2b3a67"    # the convex set / region fill
EDGE   = "#1b2440"    # region boundary
SEG    = "#c1121f"    # the segment between two points / a violation
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"    # hyperplanes, highlights
GREY   = "#6c757d"
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the first article of the **convexity spine**
> of the Optimization track. It assumes [OPTM-00 *What Is an Optimization Problem?*] — in
> particular the **feasible set** $\mathcal{C}$, the region of allowed choices in
> $\min_x f(x)$ subject to $x\in\mathcal{C}$. From the Geometry-of-Data track it borrows
> only the vocabulary of **vectors, linear combinations, and the dot product**; those are
> *used*, not rebuilt here. Its single job is to pin down one geometric property of a
> feasible region — *convexity, "no dents"* — and to show why that property is the dividing
> line that decides whether an optimization problem is tractable. It sets up
> [OPTM-04 *Convex Functions*] (where a function is called convex exactly when the region
> *above its graph* is a convex set), the constrained-optimization articles
> [OPTM-13]–[OPTM-15] (whose feasible sets are convex), and [OPTM-16] (projection onto a
> convex set). The **separating-hyperplane** picture introduced at the end is the seed of
> duality in [OPTM-15] and of the SVM margin in the Classification track.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

A set is **convex** when the straight segment joining any two of its points never leaves
the set — geometrically, the set has *no dents, no holes, no notches*. This article makes
that one-line idea precise, starting from the definition of a line segment as a
**convex combination** $(1-t)x + t y$, and then follows it to the place it earns its
keep: convexity is the property of a feasible region that lets a *local* search find the
*global* best, the reason the whole field of optimization splits into "convex" (solvable,
reliably) and "non-convex" (hard, in general). We build the standard convex sets a data
scientist actually meets — **half-spaces**, **balls**, **polyhedra** (the feasible set of
any system of linear inequalities) — and the standard non-examples (a crescent, a union of
two disks, the set of sparse vectors). We then prove the two **operations that preserve
convexity** — *intersection* and *affine image* — which together explain why the feasible
set of a linear program is automatically convex without checking it by hand. We close with
the **separating-hyperplane** idea: two disjoint convex sets can always be cleanly split by
a flat wall, the geometric fact that later becomes duality and the support-vector margin.
The single claim this article defends is that **convexity is not a technical nicety bolted
onto optimization — it is the structural property that makes optimization *work*, and
recognizing it (or its absence) is the first applicability call a practitioner makes.**

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
A set $\mathcal{C}$ is **convex** if, for *every* pair of points $x,y\in\mathcal{C}$, the
entire line segment between them stays inside $\mathcal{C}$:
$$
x,y\in\mathcal{C}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
(1-t)\,x + t\,y \in \mathcal{C}
\quad\text{for all } t\in[0,1].
$$
In one phrase: **you can always see from any point to any other in a straight line without
leaving the set** — no dents, no holes. This is the property that turns optimization from
hard to tractable: over a convex region, "downhill in every direction" can only mean you
have reached the *global* bottom, so a local search cannot be fooled. Convexity is
preserved by **intersection** and by **affine maps**, which is why the feasible set of any
collection of linear constraints is automatically convex.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The definition will look almost too simple to matter.
The work of the article is to convince you that this single inequality — *the segment
stays inside* — is the exact hinge on which the difficulty of an optimization problem
turns, and to give you the fluency to look at a feasible region and call it convex or not
on sight.

# Why we are here: the property that decides "easy" from "hard" {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **why does optimization care so much about
the *shape* of the feasible region, and why is "no dents" the shape that makes everything
work?** Every definition and proof below is an answer to one piece of that question.

## What the earlier articles left open

[OPTM-01] and [OPTM-02] taught the *local* test for a minimum: find where the derivative
(or the gradient) is zero, then check the curvature. That test is genuinely local — it
inspects the function in an infinitesimal neighborhood of a point and pronounces "this is a
minimum." But a minimum *of what scope*? The second-derivative test can only ever certify a
**local** minimum: the lowest point *in some small neighborhood*. It is completely silent
about whether some far-away valley is lower still.

This is not a pedantic worry. Almost every model in the rest of the series is fit by
minimizing something, and what we actually want is the *global* best parameters, not merely
a point that looks good to its immediate neighbors. So a gap opened in [OPTM-02] that we
never closed: **when is a local minimum guaranteed to be the global one?** When can we trust
that "downhill in every direction" means "the bottom," full stop?

The answer turns out to have two halves, and they are the two articles of this spine. One
half is a property of the *function* being minimized (convex functions, [OPTM-04]). The
other — the one we tackle first, here — is a property of the *region* we are minimizing
over. That property is **convexity of the feasible set**, and it is what this article is
about.

## A picture of why dents are fatal {#sec-why-dents}

Here is the intuition, before any symbols, for why the *shape* of the region matters.
Imagine you are standing inside a region, trying to walk to its lowest point (lowest by
some objective), and your only rule is "take a step in a downhill direction that stays
inside the region." If the region is a nice solid blob, this works: keep stepping downhill
and you reach the bottom. But suppose the region has a **dent** — a notch carved into it,
like a kidney bean or a crescent. Then you can get *trapped*. You walk downhill into a tip
of the crescent, and to reach a genuinely lower point on the *other* tip you would have to
walk *uphill first*, or step *outside* the region across the notch. A purely local
"downhill and stay inside" rule cannot do either. The dent has manufactured a fake bottom.

@fig-dent-trap shows exactly this. The whole reason we are about to spend an article on a
geometric definition is that **the dent is the enemy of local search**, and convexity is
precisely "no dents."

```{python}
#| label: fig-dent-trap
#| fig-cap: "Why a dent is fatal to local search. **Left:** a convex region — any straight path between two interior points stays inside, so following a downhill direction can only end at the one true bottom. **Right:** a crescent (non-convex). The straight segment between the two marked points (red) cuts *outside* the region across the notch. A local 'step downhill but stay inside' rule walking into the upper tip is trapped: the lower tip is unreachable without first going up or leaving. The dent fabricates a local minimum that is not global."
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.6, 4.0))

# Left: a convex blob (a disk) with an interior chord.
th = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 200)
axL.fill(np.cos(th), np.sin(th), color=REGION, alpha=0.35, edgecolor=EDGE, lw=2)
p, q = np.array([-0.7, 0.3]), np.array([0.6, -0.5])
axL.plot([p[0], q[0]], [p[1], q[1]], color=SEG, lw=2.5, zorder=3)
axL.scatter(*np.array([p, q]).T, color=SEG, s=55, zorder=4)
axL.set_title("Convex: segment stays inside")
axL.set_xlim(-1.4, 1.4); axL.set_ylim(-1.4, 1.4); axL.set_aspect("equal")
axL.set_xticks([]); axL.set_yticks([])

# Right: a crescent = big disk minus a shifted disk.
gx, gy = np.meshgrid(np.linspace(-1.5, 1.7, 400), np.linspace(-1.5, 1.5, 400))
inbig = gx**2 + gy**2 <= 1.2**2
incut = (gx - 0.85)**2 + gy**2 <= 1.0**2
crescent = inbig & ~incut
axR.contourf(gx, gy, crescent.astype(float), levels=[0.5, 1.5], colors=[REGION], alpha=0.35)
axR.contour(gx, gy, crescent.astype(float), levels=[0.5], colors=[EDGE], linewidths=2)
a, b = np.array([-0.9, 0.55]), np.array([-0.9, -0.55])
axR.plot([a[0], b[0]], [a[1], b[1]], color=SEG, lw=2.5, zorder=3)
axR.scatter(*np.array([a, b]).T, color=SEG, s=55, zorder=4)
axR.set_title("Non-convex: segment leaves the set")
axR.set_xlim(-1.5, 1.7); axR.set_ylim(-1.5, 1.5); axR.set_aspect("equal")
axR.set_xticks([]); axR.set_yticks([])
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"In one sentence, why does convexity matter in optimization?"* Because over a convex
feasible set, with a convex objective, **every local minimum is automatically a global
minimum** — so a local search (gradient descent, Newton's method) provably finds the right
answer, and you never have to worry about being trapped in a false valley. That single
guarantee, proved in [OPTM-04], is the entire payoff, and it rests on the feasible region
being convex (this article) *and* the objective being convex (the next one). Non-convex
problems lose the guarantee: the best you can certify is a local minimum, which is why
training a neural network is fundamentally harder than fitting a linear model.
:::

## A one-paragraph history

The systematic study of convex sets is younger than calculus. Hermann Minkowski, around
1900, built the geometry of convex bodies — support functions, the separating hyperplane,
mixed volumes — originally for number theory. The separating-hyperplane theorems were
sharpened in the 1900s–1940s (Minkowski, then Hahn and Banach in infinite dimensions). The
payoff for *optimization* arrived with linear programming: George Dantzig's simplex method
(1947) optimizes a linear objective over a polyhedron — a convex set — and its correctness
is a fact about convex geometry. Convex *analysis* as a unified field is largely the work of
Werner Fenchel (1950s) and R. Tyrrell Rockafellar (whose 1970 *Convex Analysis* is the
standard reference); modern convex optimization as practiced in data science is codified in
Boyd and Vandenberghe (2004). The thread running through all of it is the one in
@sec-why-dents: convexity is the structural assumption that makes "find the best" reliably
answerable.

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Before the formal definition, fix the one geometric object the whole article rests on: the
**straight line segment between two points**.

Take two points $x$ and $y$ in the plane (or in any number of dimensions — the picture is
the same). As you slide from $x$ to $y$ along the straight line connecting them, your
position is a *blend* of the two endpoints: start all-$x$, end all-$y$, and in between, a
weighted mix. Write the mixing weight as $t$, running from $0$ to $1$. At weight $t$ your
position is
$$
(1-t)\,x + t\,y.
$$
Check the endpoints: at $t=0$ this is $x$ (all of $x$, none of $y$); at $t=1$ it is $y$;
at $t=\tfrac12$ it is the midpoint $\tfrac12 x + \tfrac12 y$. As $t$ sweeps $[0,1]$ the
expression traces out *exactly* the segment from $x$ to $y$ and nothing more. That is what a
segment **is**, written in vector arithmetic. @fig-segment draws it.

```{python}
#| label: fig-segment
#| fig-cap: "A line segment as a blend of its endpoints. Each point on the segment from $x$ to $y$ is $(1-t)x + ty$ for some $t\\in[0,1]$: the weight $t$ slides the blend from all-$x$ ($t=0$) to all-$y$ ($t=1$), passing through the midpoint at $t=\\tfrac12$. The weights are non-negative and sum to one — this is what makes it a *convex* combination, and the segment is the set of all of them."
x = np.array([0.5, 0.6]); y = np.array([3.4, 2.3])
ts = np.array([0.0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0])
pts = np.array([(1-t)*x + t*y for t in ts])

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot([x[0], y[0]], [x[1], y[1]], color=REGION, lw=2.5, zorder=1)
ax.scatter(pts[:, 0], pts[:, 1], color=SEG, s=55, zorder=3)
for t, p in zip(ts, pts):
    ax.annotate(f"$t={t:g}$", p, textcoords="offset points", xytext=(6, 8), fontsize=9)
ax.scatter(*x, color=ACCENT, s=90, zorder=4); ax.annotate("$x$", x, textcoords="offset points", xytext=(-16, -4), fontsize=12)
ax.scatter(*y, color=ACCENT, s=90, zorder=4); ax.annotate("$y$", y, textcoords="offset points", xytext=(8, -2), fontsize=12)
ax.set_xlim(0, 4); ax.set_ylim(0, 3); ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.set_title("The segment is all blends $(1-t)x + ty$")
ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])
plt.tight_layout()
```

Now the definition of convexity is just a sentence about this object: a set is convex when,
no matter which two of its points you pick, *every* blend between them is also in the set.
The set is "closed under blending." With the segment understood, the rest is precise
bookkeeping.

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## Convex combinations and the definition {#sec-defn}

::: {#def-convex-comb}
**Definition (convex combination).** Given points $x_1,\dots,x_k$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$, a
**convex combination** of them is a weighted sum
$$
\theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \cdots + \theta_k x_k,
\qquad
\theta_i \ge 0 \ \text{for all } i,
\qquad
\sum_{i=1}^k \theta_i = 1.
$$
The two requirements — weights **non-negative** and weights **summing to one** — are what
make it *convex* rather than an arbitrary linear combination.
:::

The two conditions are doing specific geometric work, and it is worth naming what each one
forbids:

- **Weights sum to one ($\sum\theta_i = 1$).** This is what keeps the blend an *average*.
  If the weights summed to something else, the "blend" of $x$ with itself
  ($\theta x + \theta x$) would drift away from $x$. Summing to one pins the combination
  inside the *affine* span — the flat plane/line through the points — rather than letting it
  scale off toward the origin.
- **Weights non-negative ($\theta_i\ge 0$).** This is what keeps the blend *between* the
  points rather than *beyond* them. With $k=2$, writing $\theta_1 = 1-t$ and $\theta_2 = t$,
  non-negativity forces $t\in[0,1]$ — you stay on the segment. Allow a negative weight and
  you shoot off past an endpoint along the extended line.

For two points the convex combinations are exactly the segment of @fig-segment. For three
points (not all on one line) they fill the solid triangle with those corners; for $k$ points
they fill the **convex hull**, the smallest convex set containing them — the shape a rubber
band snaps to around a scatter of pins.

::: {#def-convex-set}
**Definition (convex set).** A set $\mathcal{C}\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n$ is **convex** if for
every $x,y\in\mathcal{C}$ and every $t\in[0,1]$,
$$
(1-t)\,x + t\,y \;\in\; \mathcal{C}.
$$
Equivalently: $\mathcal{C}$ contains every convex combination of *any two* of its points.
By convention the empty set and any single point are convex (there is nothing to violate).
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"The definition only mentions two points and a segment. Do I also need to check
combinations of three, four, or more points?"* No — and this is worth being able to argue.
If a set contains the segment between any two of its points, it automatically contains every
convex combination of *any* finite number of its points. The reason is that a $k$-point
convex combination can be built up two points at a time. Take
$z = \theta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \theta_k x_k$ with the $\theta_i\ge 0$ summing to one, and
assume $\theta_k < 1$ (if $\theta_k = 1$ then $z = x_k\in\mathcal{C}$ and we are done). Peel
off the last point:
$$
z = (1-\theta_k)\underbrace{\left[\tfrac{\theta_1}{1-\theta_k}x_1 + \cdots + \tfrac{\theta_{k-1}}{1-\theta_k}x_{k-1}\right]}_{\textstyle w} + \theta_k\,x_k.
$$
The new weights $\theta_i/(1-\theta_k)$ are non-negative and sum to
$\frac{\theta_1+\cdots+\theta_{k-1}}{1-\theta_k} = \frac{1-\theta_k}{1-\theta_k}=1$, so $w$
is a convex combination of *fewer* points; by induction $w\in\mathcal{C}$. Then
$z = (1-\theta_k)w + \theta_k x_k$ is a two-point convex combination of $w$ and $x_k$, both
in $\mathcal{C}$, hence in $\mathcal{C}$ by the definition. So the two-point condition is the
whole story — a genuinely convenient fact, because it means *checking* convexity only ever
involves a single segment.
:::

## Examples you will actually meet {#sec-examples-sets}

The definition is abstract; the value is in recognizing convex sets on sight. Here are the
ones that appear constantly in data science and optimization, each verified directly from
@def-convex-set.

**The whole space $\mathbb{R}^n$, and any line or plane through it.** Convex for the most
direct reason there is: a blend of two points of $\mathbb{R}^n$ is a vector in $\mathbb{R}^n$,
so the segment never has anywhere to escape *to*. More usefully, an **affine set**
— a solution set of linear equations $\{x : Ax = b\}$, e.g. a line or a plane — is convex.
If $Ax=b$ and $Ay=b$, then for any $t$,
$$
A\big((1-t)x + ty\big) = (1-t)Ax + t\,Ay = (1-t)b + t\,b = b,
$$
using only that matrix-times-vector distributes over addition and pulls out scalars. The
blend still satisfies the equations, so it is in the set.

**A half-space.** Pick a direction (a nonzero vector $a$) and a threshold $b$. The set of
points on one side of the flat wall perpendicular to $a$,
$$
H^- = \{\,x : a^\top x \le b\,\},
$$
is a **half-space**. It is convex, and we will prove it carefully in @sec-halfspace because
half-spaces are the atoms from which most useful convex sets are built — every linear
inequality constraint carves out exactly one.

**A ball.** The set of points within radius $r$ of a center $x_0$,
$$
B = \{\,x : \|x - x_0\| \le r\,\},
$$
is convex. Take $x,y$ with $\|x-x_0\|\le r$ and $\|y-x_0\|\le r$, and blend. Subtract the
center and use that the norm of a sum is at most the sum of the norms (the **triangle
inequality**, $\|u+v\|\le\|u\|+\|v\|$, an imported fact about distance), together with
$\|c u\| = |c|\,\|u\|$:
$$
\big\|\,(1-t)x + ty - x_0\,\big\|
= \big\|\,(1-t)(x-x_0) + t(y-x_0)\,\big\|
\le (1-t)\|x-x_0\| + t\|y-x_0\|
\le (1-t)r + t r = r.
$$
The first line just rewrites $x_0 = (1-t)x_0 + tx_0$ and regroups; the inequalities use the
triangle inequality and $0\le t\le 1$. So the blend is within radius $r$ — the ball is
convex.

**A polyhedron.** The feasible set of a *system* of linear inequalities,
$$
P = \{\,x : a_1^\top x \le b_1,\ \dots,\ a_m^\top x \le b_m\,\},
$$
is a **polyhedron** (a polygon in 2-D, a polytope when bounded). This is the single most
important convex set in applied optimization: it is the feasible region of every linear
program, and the constraint set of countless real problems ("spend no more than the budget,
use no less than the minimum, keep every quantity non-negative"). We will see in
@sec-intersection that it is convex *because it is an intersection of half-spaces* — no
separate proof needed.

@fig-convex-zoo draws the convex menagerie.

```{python}
#| label: fig-convex-zoo
#| fig-cap: "Convex sets a practitioner meets. A half-space (everything on one side of a line), a ball (a disk), and a polyhedron (here a polygon, the intersection of five half-spaces). In each, any segment between two points of the set stays inside — the defining property."
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(9.0, 3.3))

# Half-space a^T x <= b with a=(1,1), b=2.
ax = axes[0]
gx, gy = np.meshgrid(np.linspace(-1, 4, 300), np.linspace(-1, 4, 300))
ax.contourf(gx, gy, (gx + gy <= 2).astype(float), levels=[0.5, 1.5], colors=[REGION], alpha=0.35)
xs = np.linspace(-1, 4, 50)
ax.plot(xs, 2 - xs, color=EDGE, lw=2)
ax.set_title(r"Half-space $a^\top x \leq b$")
ax.set_xlim(-1, 4); ax.set_ylim(-1, 4); ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])

# Ball.
ax = axes[1]
th = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 200)
ax.fill(1.5 + 1.2*np.cos(th), 1.5 + 1.2*np.sin(th), color=REGION, alpha=0.35, edgecolor=EDGE, lw=2)
ax.set_title(r"Ball $\|x - x_0\| \leq r$")
ax.set_xlim(-0.3, 3.3); ax.set_ylim(-0.3, 3.3); ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])

# Polyhedron: a convex polygon.
ax = axes[2]
verts = np.array([[0.4, 0.6], [2.4, 0.3], [3.2, 1.7], [2.0, 3.0], [0.5, 2.2]])
ax.fill(verts[:, 0], verts[:, 1], color=REGION, alpha=0.35, edgecolor=EDGE, lw=2)
ax.set_title("Polyhedron (∩ of half-spaces)")
ax.set_xlim(-0.2, 3.6); ax.set_ylim(-0.2, 3.4); ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Non-examples: what convexity rules out {#sec-nonexamples}

Equally important is recognizing what is *not* convex, because the non-convex feasible set
is the one that will quietly defeat your optimizer. A set fails to be convex the moment you
can find **one** pair of points whose connecting segment pokes outside it.

- **A crescent** (@fig-dent-trap, right): the segment between the two horns leaves the set.
- **A union of two disjoint disks.** Pick a point in each disk; the segment between them
  crosses the empty gap. The union of two convex sets is *almost never* convex — a fact worth
  carrying, because it is the reason "or" constraints ("the point is in region A *or* region
  B") destroy convexity.
- **An annulus (a ring).** A segment across the hole in the middle leaves the set.
- **The set of sparse vectors** — vectors with at most $k$ nonzero entries. In two
  dimensions, "at most one nonzero entry" is the union of the two coordinate axes; the
  segment from $(1,0)$ to $(0,1)$ passes through $(\tfrac12,\tfrac12)$, which has *two*
  nonzeros and is not in the set. This is exactly why the natural sparsity constraint
  ($\|\beta\|_0\le k$) is non-convex and intractable, and why the lasso replaces it with the
  convex $\ell_1$ ball — a substitution the Regression track makes and that
  [OPTM-04]–[OPTM-05] justify.
- **The integers, or any grid.** Between $0$ and $1$ lies $\tfrac12$, not an integer.
  Integrality is non-convex, which is the deep reason integer programming is hard while its
  continuous (linear-programming) relaxation is easy.

```{python}
#| label: fig-nonconvex-zoo
#| fig-cap: "Non-convex sets. The union of two disks, an annulus, and the 'at most one nonzero coordinate' sparse set (the two axes). In each panel a single red segment between two points of the set escapes it — one witness is all it takes to break convexity. These are not exotic: 'A or B' constraints, ring-shaped feasible regions, and sparsity constraints all appear in real problems, and all are non-convex."
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(9.0, 3.3))

# Union of two disks.
ax = axes[0]
th = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 200)
ax.fill(-0.9 + 0.8*np.cos(th), 1.5 + 0.8*np.sin(th), color=REGION, alpha=0.35, edgecolor=EDGE, lw=2)
ax.fill(1.4 + 0.8*np.cos(th), 1.5 + 0.8*np.sin(th), color=REGION, alpha=0.35, edgecolor=EDGE, lw=2)
ax.plot([-0.9, 1.4], [1.5, 1.5], color=SEG, lw=2.5, zorder=3)
ax.scatter([-0.9, 1.4], [1.5, 1.5], color=SEG, s=45, zorder=4)
ax.set_title("Union of two disks")
ax.set_xlim(-2, 2.5); ax.set_ylim(0, 3); ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])

# Annulus.
ax = axes[1]
gx, gy = np.meshgrid(np.linspace(-1.6, 1.6, 400), np.linspace(-1.6, 1.6, 400))
ring = (gx**2 + gy**2 <= 1.4**2) & (gx**2 + gy**2 >= 0.6**2)
ax.contourf(gx, gy, ring.astype(float), levels=[0.5, 1.5], colors=[REGION], alpha=0.35)
ax.plot([-1.1, 1.1], [0.6, 0.6], color=SEG, lw=2.5, zorder=3)
ax.scatter([-1.1, 1.1], [0.6, 0.6], color=SEG, s=45, zorder=4)
ax.set_title("Annulus (a ring)")
ax.set_xlim(-1.7, 1.7); ax.set_ylim(-1.7, 1.7); ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])

# Sparse set: the two axes.
ax = axes[2]
ax.plot([-1.5, 1.5], [0, 0], color=REGION, lw=4, alpha=0.6)
ax.plot([0, 0], [-1.5, 1.5], color=REGION, lw=4, alpha=0.6)
ax.plot([1.2, 0], [0, 1.2], color=SEG, lw=2.5, zorder=3)
ax.scatter([1.2, 0, 0.6], [0, 1.2, 0.6], color=[SEG, SEG, "black"], s=50, zorder=4)
ax.annotate(r"$(\frac{1}{2},\frac{1}{2})$ has 2 nonzeros", (0.6, 0.6),
            textcoords="offset points", xytext=(8, 6), fontsize=8)
ax.set_title(r"$\leq 1$ nonzero coordinate")
ax.set_xlim(-1.6, 1.6); ax.set_ylim(-1.6, 1.6); ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**A set being "round" or "smooth" is neither necessary nor sufficient for convexity.** A
square (sharp corners) is convex; a smooth crescent (no corners at all) is not. Convexity is
about *segments staying inside*, not about the boundary being curved or smooth. Likewise,
do not assume the *union* of convex sets is convex — it almost never is. The reliable test is
always the same: can you find two points whose connecting segment leaves the set? If yes, not
convex; if you can prove no such pair exists, convex.
:::

## The half-space, proved {#sec-halfspace}

Half-spaces deserve their own proof because they are the building block of every polyhedron,
and because the proof is a clean template for "this linear-constraint set is convex."

::: {#lem-halfspace}
**Lemma (half-spaces are convex).** For any nonzero $a\in\mathbb{R}^n$ and any
$b\in\mathbb{R}$, the half-space $H^- = \{x : a^\top x \le b\}$ is convex. The same holds for
$\{x : a^\top x \ge b\}$ and for the hyperplane $\{x : a^\top x = b\}$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Take any $x,y\in H^-$, so $a^\top x \le b$ and $a^\top y \le b$, and any $t\in[0,1]$. We must
show the blend $(1-t)x + ty$ also satisfies the constraint. Compute $a^\top$ applied to the
blend, using only that the dot product is **linear** — it distributes over sums and pulls out
scalars, $a^\top(u+v) = a^\top u + a^\top v$ and $a^\top(cu) = c\,a^\top u$:
$$
a^\top\big((1-t)x + ty\big)
= (1-t)\,a^\top x + t\,a^\top y.
$$
Now bound each piece. Since $t\in[0,1]$, both $(1-t)$ and $t$ are $\ge 0$, so multiplying the
true inequalities $a^\top x \le b$ and $a^\top y\le b$ by these non-negative weights preserves
their direction:
$$
(1-t)\,a^\top x \le (1-t)\,b,
\qquad
t\,a^\top y \le t\,b.
$$
Add the two:
$$
a^\top\big((1-t)x + ty\big) = (1-t)\,a^\top x + t\,a^\top y \le (1-t)b + tb = b.
$$
So the blend satisfies $a^\top z\le b$, i.e. $z\in H^-$. As $x,y,t$ were arbitrary, $H^-$ is
convex. For $\{a^\top x\ge b\}$ run the identical argument with the inequality flipped; the
hyperplane $\{a^\top x = b\}$ is the intersection of the two half-spaces $\{a^\top x\le b\}$
and $\{a^\top x\ge b\}$, hence convex by @sec-intersection below (or directly: equalities are
preserved by blending, exactly as in the affine-set example of @sec-examples-sets). $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The crucial line of the proof is "multiplying a true inequality by a non-negative number
keeps it true." That is exactly where the requirement $t\in[0,1]$ — the non-negativity of the
convex-combination weights — earns its place. If we allowed a negative weight, the inequality
could flip, and the blend could land on the wrong side. This is the algebraic shadow of the
geometric fact from @sec-defn: negative weights let you escape the segment.

# Operations that preserve convexity {#sec-operations}

Checking convexity from scratch every time would be miserable. The practical power of convex
analysis is a small calculus of **operations that take convex sets to convex sets**, so you
can build complicated feasible regions from simple atoms and *know* the result is convex
without re-proving anything. We establish the two that matter most.

## Intersection {#sec-intersection}

::: {#thm-intersection}
**Theorem (intersection preserves convexity).** If $\mathcal{C}_1, \mathcal{C}_2, \dots$ is
any collection of convex sets (finitely or infinitely many), then their intersection
$\mathcal{C} = \bigcap_\alpha \mathcal{C}_\alpha$ is convex.
:::

::: {.proof}
Take any two points $x,y$ in the intersection $\mathcal{C}$, and any $t\in[0,1]$. By
definition of intersection, $x$ and $y$ lie in *every* set $\mathcal{C}_\alpha$
simultaneously. Fix one of them, $\mathcal{C}_\alpha$. Since $x,y\in\mathcal{C}_\alpha$ and
$\mathcal{C}_\alpha$ is convex, the blend $(1-t)x + ty$ lies in $\mathcal{C}_\alpha$. But this
holds for *every* $\alpha$ — each set is convex and contains both points — so the blend lies
in every $\mathcal{C}_\alpha$, which is precisely the statement that it lies in the
intersection $\mathcal{C}$. As $x,y,t$ were arbitrary, $\mathcal{C}$ is convex. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This short proof carries enormous weight. It immediately explains the most important example
of @sec-examples-sets:

> **A polyhedron is convex because it is an intersection of half-spaces.** Each constraint
> $a_i^\top x \le b_i$ defines one half-space, convex by @lem-halfspace; the polyhedron
> $P = \{x : a_i^\top x \le b_i,\ i=1,\dots,m\}$ is the set of points satisfying *all* of
> them at once, i.e. $P = \bigcap_i \{x : a_i^\top x\le b_i\}$, and by @thm-intersection an
> intersection of convex sets is convex.

So *any* feasible set described by a list of linear inequality (and equality) constraints is
automatically convex — no per-problem check required. @fig-intersection shows three
half-spaces intersecting into a convex wedge.

```{python}
#| label: fig-intersection
#| fig-cap: "Intersection preserves convexity. Three half-spaces (each bounded by a line) overlap; the dark region satisfies all three constraints at once and is their intersection — a convex polygon. Because each half-space is convex and intersection preserves convexity, the feasible set of *any* system of linear inequalities is convex by construction, with no separate proof."
gx, gy = np.meshgrid(np.linspace(-1, 5, 500), np.linspace(-1, 5, 500))
# Three constraints: x>=0.3, y>=0.3, x+y<=4 (rewritten as half-spaces).
c1 = gx >= 0.3
c2 = gy >= 0.3
c3 = gx + gy <= 4
feas = c1 & c2 & c3

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.2, 4.6))
# Shade each half-space faintly, then the intersection darkly.
for c in (c1, c2, c3):
    ax.contourf(gx, gy, c.astype(float), levels=[0.5, 1.5], colors=[GREY], alpha=0.12)
ax.contourf(gx, gy, feas.astype(float), levels=[0.5, 1.5], colors=[REGION], alpha=0.55)
# Boundary lines.
xs = np.linspace(-1, 5, 50)
ax.axvline(0.3, color=ACCENT, lw=1.6); ax.axhline(0.3, color=ACCENT, lw=1.6)
ax.plot(xs, 4 - xs, color=ACCENT, lw=1.6)
ax.set_title("∩ of three half-spaces is convex")
ax.set_xlim(-1, 5); ax.set_ylim(-1, 5); ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); ax.set_ylabel("$x_2$")
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Intersection preserves convexity — does union?"* No, and being able to say *why* in one
breath signals you understand the definition rather than memorizing a list. The intersection
proof worked because a point in the intersection is in *every* set, so the blend inherits
membership from each. For a union, a point is in *at least one* set — but the two endpoints
might live in different members of the union, and the segment between them can cross the gap.
The union-of-two-disks non-example (@fig-nonconvex-zoo) is the standard counterexample. The
clean summary: convex sets are closed under **intersection ("and")** but not under **union
("or")** — which is exactly why "and"-constraints keep problems convex and "or"-constraints
break them.
:::

## Affine image {#sec-affine}

The second preserving operation is the **affine map** — the stretch-rotate-shift $x\mapsto
Ax+b$ that linear algebra runs on. We need a small fact first.

A map $f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^m$ of the form $f(x) = Ax + b$ (with $A$ an $m\times n$
matrix and $b\in\mathbb{R}^m$) is called **affine** — linear, plus a constant shift. The one
property we use is that **an affine map sends a blend to the blend of the images**: for any
$x,y$ and $t\in[0,1]$,
$$
f\big((1-t)x + ty\big)
= A\big((1-t)x + ty\big) + b
= (1-t)Ax + t\,Ay + b.
$$
Now split the constant $b = (1-t)b + tb$ (legal because $(1-t)+t = 1$ — here is where
"weights sum to one" pays off) and regroup:
$$
(1-t)Ax + tAy + (1-t)b + tb
= (1-t)(Ax + b) + t(Ay + b)
= (1-t)f(x) + t\,f(y).
$$
So $f\big((1-t)x + ty\big) = (1-t)f(x) + tf(y)$: applying $f$ to a point on the segment lands
you on the segment between the images. This is the engine of the next theorem.

::: {#thm-affine}
**Theorem (affine maps preserve convexity).** Let $f(x) = Ax + b$ be affine and let
$\mathcal{C}\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n$ be convex. Then the **image**
$f(\mathcal{C}) = \{\,Ax + b : x\in\mathcal{C}\,\}$ is convex. Conversely, the **preimage**
$f^{-1}(\mathcal{D}) = \{\,x : Ax + b\in\mathcal{D}\,\}$ of a convex set $\mathcal{D}$ is
convex.
:::

::: {.proof}
*Image.* Take any two points $u,v$ in $f(\mathcal{C})$. By definition of the image, each is
$f$ of some source point: $u = f(x)$ and $v = f(y)$ for some $x,y\in\mathcal{C}$. Fix
$t\in[0,1]$ and consider the blend $(1-t)u + tv$. Using the boxed property above *in reverse*,
$$
(1-t)u + tv = (1-t)f(x) + t\,f(y) = f\big((1-t)x + ty\big).
$$
The point $(1-t)x + ty$ lies in $\mathcal{C}$ because $\mathcal{C}$ is convex and
$x,y\in\mathcal{C}$. Therefore its image under $f$ lies in $f(\mathcal{C})$. But that image is
exactly $(1-t)u + tv$. So the blend of $u$ and $v$ is in $f(\mathcal{C})$; as $u,v,t$ were
arbitrary, $f(\mathcal{C})$ is convex.

*Preimage.* Take any $x,y$ in $f^{-1}(\mathcal{D})$, meaning $f(x)\in\mathcal{D}$ and
$f(y)\in\mathcal{D}$, and any $t\in[0,1]$. By the boxed property,
$$
f\big((1-t)x + ty\big) = (1-t)f(x) + t\,f(y),
$$
which is a blend of two points $f(x),f(y)$ of the convex set $\mathcal{D}$, hence lies in
$\mathcal{D}$. So $(1-t)x + ty$ maps into $\mathcal{D}$, i.e. it belongs to
$f^{-1}(\mathcal{D})$. Thus $f^{-1}(\mathcal{D})$ is convex. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The preimage half is quietly the more useful one. It says: **if you describe a feasible set
by passing a linear function through a convex constraint, the set is convex.** For instance,
$\{x : Ax\le b\}$ — the polyhedron again — is the preimage of the convex "non-positive
orthant" $\{z : z \le b\}$ under the affine map $x\mapsto Ax$, so it is convex. And it
explains why the **probability simplex** $\{\theta : \theta_i\ge 0,\ \sum_i\theta_i = 1\}$ —
the set of all probability distributions over $n$ outcomes, ubiquitous in classification and
mixture models — is convex: it is the intersection of the non-negative orthant (convex,
half-spaces) with the affine hyperplane $\sum_i\theta_i = 1$ (convex), so it is convex by
@thm-intersection.

@fig-affine shows a convex polygon stretched and rotated by an affine map, staying convex.

```{python}
#| label: fig-affine
#| fig-cap: "An affine map preserves convexity. **Left:** a convex polygon $\\mathcal{C}$. **Right:** its image under $f(x)=Ax+b$ (a rotation, a stretch, and a shift). The shape is sheared, but it stays convex — straight segments map to straight segments, so 'no dents' is inherited. This is why you can build a convex feasible set in convenient coordinates and transform it freely."
verts = np.array([[0.4, 0.6], [2.4, 0.3], [3.2, 1.7], [2.0, 3.0], [0.5, 2.2]])
A = np.array([[0.8, -0.5], [0.4, 0.9]])
b = np.array([1.0, -0.5])
img = verts @ A.T + b

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.6, 3.8))
axL.fill(verts[:, 0], verts[:, 1], color=REGION, alpha=0.35, edgecolor=EDGE, lw=2)
axL.set_title(r"Convex set $\mathcal{C}$")
axL.set_xlim(-1, 4); axL.set_ylim(-2, 4); axL.set_aspect("equal"); axL.set_xticks([]); axL.set_yticks([])

axR.fill(img[:, 0], img[:, 1], color=ACCENT, alpha=0.35, edgecolor="#06502f", lw=2)
axR.set_title(r"Affine image $A\mathcal{C}+b$ (still convex)")
axR.set_xlim(-1, 4); axR.set_ylim(-2, 4); axR.set_aspect("equal"); axR.set_xticks([]); axR.set_yticks([])
plt.tight_layout()
```

There are more preserving operations — scaling, sums, Cartesian products, perspective maps —
but intersection and affine image are the two that carry almost all the practical weight, and
they are the two the rest of the track will lean on. The others are built the same way (find
the right blend identity), and we will introduce any we need when we need them.

# Hyperplanes and the separating-hyperplane idea {#sec-separating}

We now reach the geometric fact that makes convexity *do something* far beyond "local search
works": two convex sets that do not overlap can always be cleanly split by a flat wall. This
is the seed of duality ([OPTM-15]) and of the support-vector machine's margin (Classification
track), so it is worth meeting now even though we only state it geometrically.

## Hyperplanes and half-spaces, named precisely {#sec-hyperplane}

A **hyperplane** is the flat $(n{-}1)$-dimensional wall
$$
H = \{\,x : a^\top x = b\,\},
\qquad a\ne 0,
$$
a line in 2-D, an ordinary plane in 3-D. The vector $a$ is the **normal** — it points
perpendicular to the wall — and $b$ fixes the wall's offset from the origin. (Why is $a$
perpendicular to $H$? If $x$ and $y$ both lie on $H$, then $a^\top x = a^\top y = b$, so
$a^\top(x-y) = 0$: the normal is orthogonal to every direction $x-y$ lying *within* the
hyperplane. That orthogonality is the dot-product fact imported from the Geometry track.)

Each hyperplane splits the whole space into the two **half-spaces** $\{a^\top x\le b\}$ and
$\{a^\top x\ge b\}$ of @lem-halfspace — the two sides of the wall. So a hyperplane is the
*boundary* and a half-space is *one side*. With this vocabulary the central theorem is one
sentence.

## The separating-hyperplane theorem {#sec-sep-thm}

::: {#thm-separating}
**Theorem (separating hyperplane).** Let $\mathcal{C}$ and $\mathcal{D}$ be two convex sets
in $\mathbb{R}^n$ that do not intersect ($\mathcal{C}\cap\mathcal{D}=\varnothing$). Then there
exist a nonzero vector $a$ and a scalar $b$ such that
$$
a^\top x \le b \ \text{ for all } x\in\mathcal{C},
\qquad
a^\top x \ge b \ \text{ for all } x\in\mathcal{D}.
$$
That is, the hyperplane $\{x : a^\top x = b\}$ has *all* of $\mathcal{C}$ on one side and
*all* of $\mathcal{D}$ on the other — a single flat wall separates them.
:::

We give the geometric argument rather than the full topological proof (the complete proof,
handling the boundary cases where the sets touch, lives in the convex-analysis references of
@sec-deeper; the construction below is the heart of it and is fully rigorous when the sets are
closed and at least one is bounded).

::: {.proof}
*(Construction, the heart of the theorem.)* Because $\mathcal{C}$ and $\mathcal{D}$ are
disjoint convex sets, there is a **closest pair**: points $c^\star\in\mathcal{C}$ and
$d^\star\in\mathcal{D}$ achieving the smallest possible distance between the two sets. (Such a
closest pair exists whenever the sets are closed and one is bounded — a fact from the same
"continuous function on a closed bounded set attains its minimum" principle used in [OPTM-00]
for the existence of minimizers.) Since the sets do not meet, $c^\star\ne d^\star$, so the gap
vector
$$
a = d^\star - c^\star \ne 0
$$
is a genuine direction — point it from $\mathcal{C}$ toward $\mathcal{D}$. Now place the wall
*squarely across the middle of the gap*, perpendicular to $a$, choosing the offset
$$
b = a^\top\!\left(\frac{c^\star + d^\star}{2}\right)
$$
so the hyperplane passes through the midpoint of the closest pair. The claim is that no point
of $\mathcal{C}$ crosses to $\mathcal{D}$'s side, and vice versa.

First record where $c^\star$ itself sits relative to the wall. Using $a = d^\star - c^\star$
and the chosen offset $b$, subtract:
$$
a^\top c^\star - b
= a^\top c^\star - a^\top\!\left(\frac{c^\star + d^\star}{2}\right)
= \tfrac12\,a^\top\big(c^\star - d^\star\big)
= \tfrac12\,a^\top(-a)
= -\tfrac12\,\|a\|^2 \;<\; 0,
$$
so $a^\top c^\star < b$: the closest point $c^\star$ lies strictly on $\mathcal{C}$'s side of
the midpoint wall, as the picture demands. (The norm-squared identity $a^\top a = \|a\|^2$ is
the definition of the Euclidean norm, imported from the Geometry track.)

Now here is why *all* of $\mathcal{C}$ stays on that side. Suppose, for contradiction, some
$x\in\mathcal{C}$ had $a^\top x > b$ — it poked past the wall toward $\mathcal{D}$. Walk a tiny
step from $c^\star$ toward $x$, landing at $c(s) = c^\star + s(x - c^\star)$ for small
$s\in[0,1]$. This stays inside $\mathcal{C}$ (it is a point on the segment $c^\star \to x$, and
$\mathcal{C}$ is convex), so its distance to $d^\star$ cannot be smaller than the
closest-pair distance. Measure that distance squared and watch it as $s$ grows from $0$.
Write $w = x - c^\star$ and $r = c^\star - d^\star = -a$, so $c(s) - d^\star = r + s\,w$, and
expand the squared norm as an ordinary quadratic in the *single* variable $s$ (using
$\|u\|^2 = u^\top u$ and that the dot product distributes):
$$
\phi(s) \;=\; \|\,c(s) - d^\star\,\|^2
\;=\; (r + s\,w)^\top(r + s\,w)
\;=\; \|r\|^2 \;+\; 2s\,(r^\top w) \;+\; s^2\,\|w\|^2.
$$
This is a plain parabola in $s$; differentiate it with Calc-1 rules:
$\phi'(s) = 2\,(r^\top w) + 2s\,\|w\|^2$, so at the starting point
$$
\phi'(0) = 2\,r^\top w = 2(c^\star - d^\star)^\top(x - c^\star) = -2\,a^\top(x - c^\star)
= -2\big(a^\top x - a^\top c^\star\big).
$$
By assumption $a^\top x > b$, and we just showed $a^\top c^\star < b$, so
$a^\top x - a^\top c^\star > 0$ and therefore $\phi'(0) < 0$: the squared distance is *strictly
decreasing* as we leave $c^\star$ toward $x$. A small enough step thus produces a point of
$\mathcal{C}$ strictly closer to $d^\star\in\mathcal{D}$ than $c^\star$ was — contradicting that
$c^\star,d^\star$ were the closest pair. The contradiction forces $a^\top x \le b$ for all
$x\in\mathcal{C}$. The identical argument with
the roles of $\mathcal{C}$ and $\mathcal{D}$ swapped gives $a^\top x \ge b$ for all
$x\in\mathcal{D}$. So the wall separates them. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The construction is the entire intuition: **find the closest pair of points across the gap,
and lay the wall across the middle of that gap, facing along the gap direction.** @fig-separate
draws it, and also shows why disjointness alone is not enough without convexity — two
*non-convex* disjoint sets (interlocking C-shapes) cannot be split by any single flat wall.

```{python}
#| label: fig-separate
#| fig-cap: "The separating hyperplane. **Left:** two disjoint *convex* sets. The closest pair of points (red) defines the gap direction $a = d^\\star - c^\\star$; the separating line (green) sits across the middle of the gap, perpendicular to $a$, with all of one set on each side. **Right:** two disjoint *non-convex* sets (interlocking shapes) — no single straight wall can separate them, showing the theorem genuinely needs convexity, not just disjointness."
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 4.2))

# Left: two disjoint convex blobs (an ellipse-ish polygon and a disk).
poly = np.array([[0.2, 1.0], [1.2, 0.4], [1.6, 1.6], [1.0, 2.6], [0.1, 2.1]])
axL.fill(poly[:, 0], poly[:, 1], color=REGION, alpha=0.35, edgecolor=EDGE, lw=2)
th = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 200)
cx, cy, r = 3.6, 1.7, 0.7
axL.fill(cx + r*np.cos(th), cy + r*np.sin(th), color=ACCENT, alpha=0.30, edgecolor="#06502f", lw=2)
# Closest pair (approx): right tip of poly toward disk center.
cstar = np.array([1.6, 1.6]); dstar = np.array([cx - r, cy])  # left edge of disk approx
mid = (cstar + dstar) / 2
a = dstar - cstar
# Separating line through mid, perpendicular to a.
perp = np.array([-a[1], a[0]]); perp = perp / np.linalg.norm(perp)
L = 1.8
p1 = mid - L*perp; p2 = mid + L*perp
axL.plot([p1[0], p2[0]], [p1[1], p2[1]], color="#06502f", lw=2.2, zorder=2)
axL.plot([cstar[0], dstar[0]], [cstar[1], dstar[1]], color=SEG, lw=1.8, ls="--", zorder=3)
axL.scatter([cstar[0], dstar[0]], [cstar[1], dstar[1]], color=SEG, s=55, zorder=4)
axL.annotate(r"$c^\star$", cstar, textcoords="offset points", xytext=(-22, 2), fontsize=11)
axL.annotate(r"$d^\star$", dstar, textcoords="offset points", xytext=(6, -4), fontsize=11)
axL.set_title("Convex + disjoint ⇒ separable")
axL.set_xlim(-0.4, 4.8); axL.set_ylim(0, 3.2); axL.set_aspect("equal"); axL.set_xticks([]); axL.set_yticks([])

# Right: two interlocking C-shapes (non-convex), not separable by a line.
def cshape(cx, cy, flip=1):
    th = np.linspace(-2.2, 2.2, 100) * flip
    outer = np.column_stack([cx + 1.0*np.cos(th), cy + 1.0*np.sin(th)])
    inner = np.column_stack([cx + 0.55*np.cos(th[::-1]), cy + 0.55*np.sin(th[::-1])])
    return np.vstack([outer, inner])
c1 = cshape(1.7, 1.6, flip=1)
c2 = cshape(2.3, 1.6, flip=-1)
axR.fill(c1[:, 0], c1[:, 1], color=REGION, alpha=0.40, edgecolor=EDGE, lw=2)
axR.fill(c2[:, 0], c2[:, 1], color=ACCENT, alpha=0.35, edgecolor="#06502f", lw=2)
axR.set_title("Non-convex: no separating line")
axR.set_xlim(0, 4); axR.set_ylim(0, 3.2); axR.set_aspect("equal"); axR.set_xticks([]); axR.set_yticks([])
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Where does the separating hyperplane actually show up in machine learning?"* Two headline
places, and naming both shows range.

1. **Support-vector machines.** A linearly separable classification problem is two convex
   sets — the convex hulls of the positive and negative training points — and the SVM's
   decision boundary is *the* separating hyperplane, chosen to be the one with the widest gap
   (the *maximum margin*). The closest-pair construction of @thm-separating is literally what
   the SVM optimizes. (Classification track owns the SVM; it links here for this geometry.)
2. **Duality.** The reason the *dual* of a convex optimization problem touches the primal
   with no gap (strong duality) is a separating-hyperplane argument applied to a cleverly
   constructed convex set in "value space." [OPTM-15] makes this precise. When data are *not*
   separable — the convex hulls overlap — the theorem's hypothesis fails, which is exactly the
   case that motivates soft-margin SVMs and the kernel trick (mapping to a higher-dimensional
   space where they *are* separable).
:::

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

The honest boundary of this article is not "when is a set convex" — that is just the
definition — but **what convexity does and does not buy you, and where the assumption
silently fails in practice.**

**Convexity of the region is only half the guarantee.** This article secured the *feasible
set*. But "local minimum = global minimum" also needs the *objective* to be convex
([OPTM-04]). A convex feasible region with a non-convex objective (e.g. minimizing a wiggly
loss over a box constraint) can still have spurious local minima — the box did not save you.
Both halves are required; do not over-claim from this article alone.

**Most real machine-learning objectives are non-convex.** The flagship case: a neural network's
loss as a function of its weights is wildly non-convex, riddled with local minima and saddle
points. Convexity is *absent*, and the whole apparatus of guaranteed global optimality is
gone. What practitioners reach for instead is honest about this: stochastic gradient descent
([OPTM-11]) that *finds a good local minimum* and relies on empirical luck and architecture,
not a global guarantee. Likewise, $k$-means clustering, Gaussian mixtures (EM), and matrix
factorization are all non-convex. The correct mental model is: **convexity is the well-lit
room where optimization is solved; much of modern ML works in the dark next door, using
convex intuition as a flashlight.**

**Convexity can be hidden, or recoverable.** Sometimes a problem looks non-convex but a change
of variables convexifies it (the log-transform of a geometric program; the SVM's lift to
feature space). The skill is recognizing that a feasible set written one way may be convex
even when "no linear constraints" suggests otherwise. Take
$\mathcal{H} = \{(x_1,x_2) : x_1 > 0,\ x_2 > 0,\ x_1 x_2 \ge 1\}$ — the region lying on or above
the hyperbola $x_2 = 1/x_1$ in the positive quadrant. The defining constraint is *nonlinear*,
so none of @sec-examples-sets applies directly; yet the set is convex, and the only honest way
to know is to run the segment test by hand. Take $x = (x_1,x_2)$ and $y = (y_1,y_2)$ in
$\mathcal{H}$ (so $x_1x_2\ge 1$, $y_1y_2\ge 1$, all four coordinates positive) and any
$t\in[0,1]$. The blend $(1-t)x + ty$ has positive coordinates (a non-negative blend of positive
numbers), so we only need its coordinate product to be $\ge 1$. Multiply the two blended
coordinates out:
$$
\big[(1-t)x_1 + ty_1\big]\big[(1-t)x_2 + ty_2\big]
= (1-t)^2\,x_1x_2 \;+\; t^2\,y_1y_2 \;+\; t(1-t)\,(x_1y_2 + y_1x_2).
$$
Bound the three pieces. The first two use the memberships directly: $x_1x_2\ge 1$ and
$y_1y_2\ge 1$. For the cross term, use the floor-level fact that for any non-negative $u,v$,
$(\sqrt{u}-\sqrt{v})^2\ge 0$ expands to $u + v \ge 2\sqrt{uv}$ (the **AM–GM inequality**).
Applying it to $u = x_1y_2$ and $v = y_1x_2$,
$$
x_1y_2 + y_1x_2 \;\ge\; 2\sqrt{x_1y_2\cdot y_1x_2}
\;=\; 2\sqrt{(x_1x_2)(y_1y_2)} \;\ge\; 2\sqrt{1\cdot 1} \;=\; 2,
$$
where the last step again used both memberships. Substituting the three bounds (and using
$t,(1-t)\ge 0$, so the inequalities survive multiplication by the weights):
$$
\big[(1-t)x_1 + ty_1\big]\big[(1-t)x_2 + ty_2\big]
\;\ge\; (1-t)^2 + t^2 + 2\,t(1-t)
\;=\; \big[(1-t) + t\big]^2 = 1.
$$
So the blend's coordinate product is at least $1$ — the blend is back in $\mathcal{H}$, and the
set is convex despite the nonlinear constraint. (The slicker route, available after [OPTM-04]:
$1/x_1$ is a convex function on $x_1>0$, and $\mathcal{H}$ is exactly the region above its graph,
which [OPTM-04] shows is convex precisely *because* the function is. The by-hand check above is
the same fact without that machinery.) The reliable test never changes: **does every segment
between two points of the set stay inside?**

**The edge case the definition quietly covers.** A set that is *almost* convex — convex except
for a single missing boundary point, or a thin notch — is *not* convex, and the failure can be
invisible in a coarse plot. The witness is always a specific pair of points whose segment
escapes; if you cannot rule out every such pair, do not claim convexity. In high dimensions,
where you cannot draw the set, this is checked algebraically (is the constraint a $\le$ of a
convex function? an intersection of such? an affine image of one?) using exactly the calculus
of @sec-operations.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**A convex feasible set does not make a problem solvable on its own.** The phrase "convex
optimization problem" requires *both* a convex feasible set (this article) *and* a convex
objective ([OPTM-04]) — and even then, "solvable in theory" is not "solvable at your scale."
Recognizing convexity is a *necessary* first applicability call, not a sufficient one. The
companion mistake is the reverse: declaring a problem hopeless because it *looks* non-convex,
when a reformulation (different variables, a relaxation, a lift) would make it convex. Both
errors come from treating convexity as a surface feature instead of checking the segment
condition on the actual set.
:::

# How you would actually check this in practice {#sec-implementation}

The math is primary; this is the secondary, practical note. You will rarely verify convexity
by hand with the segment definition once a problem is larger than a few variables. Instead:

- **Recognize the atoms and the calculus.** In practice you build the feasible set from
  convex atoms — affine equalities ($Ax=b$), linear inequalities ($Ax\le b$), norm balls
  ($\|x\|\le r$), the simplex — and combine them only by *convexity-preserving* operations
  (intersection, affine maps). If every constraint set is a recognized convex set and you only
  ever intersect, the result is convex by @thm-intersection and @thm-affine. This is exactly
  how a tool like **CVXPY** works: its *disciplined convex programming* ruleset is, under the
  hood, this calculus of preserving operations, and it *refuses to compile* a problem it cannot
  certify convex — turning "is my feasible set convex?" into a check the software performs for
  you.
- **When in doubt, test a segment numerically.** For a set defined by a constraint
  $g(x)\le 0$, sample many random pairs $x,y$ that satisfy it and check whether
  $(1-t)x + ty$ does too for a grid of $t\in[0,1]$. A single violation disproves convexity; no
  violations is evidence (not proof) of it. This is a quick sanity check, never a substitute
  for the structural argument above.

You now know what those tools are checking and why a "DCP error" is the software telling you
your feasible set (or objective) fell outside the convex calculus of @sec-operations.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[OPTM-00 *What Is an Optimization Problem?*]**, which named the **feasible
set** $\mathcal{C}$ in $\min_x f(x)$ subject to $x\in\mathcal{C}$, and from **[OPTM-02]**,
which left open *when a local minimum is global*. This article gave the first half of the
answer — the *region* must be convex — and the geometric toolkit for recognizing and building
convex sets. From here the convexity spine continues:

- **[OPTM-04 *Convex Functions*]** — the second half of the guarantee. A function is convex
  exactly when the region *above its graph* (the **epigraph**) is a convex set — so convex
  functions are *defined by* the convex sets of this article. There the headline theorem
  finally lands: over a convex set, a convex objective's every local minimum is global.
- **[OPTM-05 *Subgradients*]** — convex calculus for functions with kinks, built on the
  tangent-below picture that convexity makes available.
- **[OPTM-13]–[OPTM-15] *Constrained optimization*** — Lagrange multipliers, KKT, and
  duality, all of which assume the feasible set is convex (a polyhedron, a ball, an
  intersection of such) and whose deepest theorem (strong duality) is a
  separating-hyperplane argument (@thm-separating) in disguise.
- **[OPTM-16] *Projected and proximal methods*** — algorithms that, each step, *project* the
  iterate back onto a convex feasible set; the projection is well-defined precisely because the
  set is convex (a unique closest point — the same closest-point idea from @thm-separating).

And outside this track: the **separating hyperplane** is the geometric core of the
**support-vector machine** in the Classification track, and the convex/non-convex distinction
is the line between the well-understood models (linear/logistic regression, SVMs, lasso) and
the empirically-trained ones (deep networks, mixtures) across the whole series.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The standard modern reference.** S. Boyd and L. Vandenberghe, *Convex Optimization*
  (2004), Chapter 2 ("Convex sets") covers everything here — convex combinations, the
  operations that preserve convexity, hyperplanes, and the separating- and supporting-
  hyperplane theorems — with the full topological care we deferred. It is freely available and
  is the book the data-science world actually learns convex optimization from.
- **The foundational text.** R. T. Rockafellar, *Convex Analysis* (1970), is the rigorous
  source for separating hyperplanes, supporting hyperplanes, and the geometry that underlies
  duality. Heavier going, but definitive.
- **The historical root.** H. Minkowski's *Geometrie der Zahlen* (1896) originated the
  systematic geometry of convex bodies and the separation idea, for number theory — a reminder
  that the geometry came long before the optimization application.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *The full proof of the separating-
  hyperplane theorem in all boundary cases* (when the sets merely touch, or are both unbounded)
  — the construction here is the heart, but the edge cases need the supporting-hyperplane
  theorem and a limiting argument (Boyd–Vandenberghe §2.5). *The dual description of a convex
  set* — every closed convex set is the intersection of all half-spaces containing it, the
  "supporting hyperplane" viewpoint that makes duality ([OPTM-15]) inevitable. *Convex hulls
  and Carathéodory's theorem* (in $\mathbb{R}^n$, every point of a hull is a convex combination
  of at most $n+1$ of the original points) — the combinatorial side of convexity, which the
  Geometry-of-Data track takes up.
